method3.generated_article:
==Geography==
Kavachi is a submarine stratovolcano in the South Pacific Ocean, located in the western Solomon Islands about 24 km south of Vangunu Island.[2] It is situated in a convergent tectonic setting where the Pacific Plate subducts beneath the Solomon Plate, within the broader Pacific Ring of Fire.[1][2] The volcanic edifice rises more than 1,200 m from the seafloor, with its summit lying roughly 20–24 m below sea level.[2][4] Because the summit is close to the sea surface, eruptions can produce discoloured water plumes visible from aircraft and satellites.[2] Observations in 2015 described Kavachi as having an elongated crater measuring about 120 m by 75 m, surrounded by a rim approximately 24 m high.[4] A secondary peak, previously only suspected, was confirmed at a depth of about 260 m below sea level.[4] Kavachi has also periodically formed temporary islands during eruptions, with some ephemeral landforms reported to have reached lengths of up to about 1 km.[1][3]

==Eruptions==
Kavachi is among the most active submarine volcanoes in the Pacific, with historically documented eruptions beginning in 1939.[2] Its shallow summit, about 20 m below sea level, allows eruptions to produce conspicuous discoloured water plumes, steam, ash, and volcanic debris at or near the sea surface.[1][4] The eruptions are commonly phreatomagmatic, caused by the interaction of magma with seawater, and can be visible from aircraft or satellites.[1][4]  

Eruptions have repeatedly built temporary islands, with at least nine island-forming episodes recorded since 1939.[2] These islands have not persisted because they were too small and insufficiently protected by solid lava flows to resist wave erosion, typically disappearing within weeks or months.[1][2] Some ephemeral islands produced by Kavachi have reportedly reached lengths of up to about 1 km.[3]  

Kavachi’s activity is episodic but frequent, with near-surface eruptions occurring every few years and discoloured water plumes observed repeatedly during active phases.[1][2] The Global Volcanism Program eruption record cited by VolcanoDiscovery lists 33 historic eruptions and indicates activity during roughly half of the years since 1939, although earlier submarine activity may have been underreported.[2] In late 1951, activity at Kavachi was associated with frequent small tsunamis.[2] A new eruptive phase began in 2021 and was reported as continuing into the mid-2020s.[2][4]

==Marine life==
During a 2015 expedition, cameras and sampling equipment recorded a biological community in Kavachi’s crater despite warm, acidic, particle-rich water affected by volcanic gases.[4] Documented organisms included gelatinous zooplankton, reef fish, microbial communities, and large sharks.[3][4] The sharks filmed in the crater included scalloped hammerheads and silky sharks, which were observed responding aggressively to baited camera systems.[4] Measurements reported from the crater area indicated temperatures up to about 13 °C above surrounding seawater, reaching approximately 42 °C, and a pH of about 6.1 in waters enriched with carbon dioxide and sulfur compounds.[4] The suspended-particle hydrothermal plume has been described as a habitat for chemosynthetic microbes, including bacteria of the genus Sulfurimonas that can use sulfur and carbon dioxide.[4] The shark observations popularized the nickname “Sharkcano”, although researchers have not established whether the animals reside there continuously or enter mainly during quiescent periods.[1][3] Suggested explanations for their presence include behavioral avoidance of eruptive phases, sensitivity to chemical or seismic warning signals, and physiological tolerance of warmer, more acidic conditions, but these hypotheses remain uncertain.[1] Kavachi is therefore regarded as a natural laboratory for studying extreme marine ecosystems, biological resilience, and the response of marine life to changing ocean chemistry.[1][3]
